query: evidence on use of radiolabeled albumin for measuring interstitial fluid volume

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Volume Study -- Specimen Collection
retrieved_docs.content: After this, intravenous access is obtained, and a control sample of blood is drawn from the patient. The patient then receives a prepared sample containing 1 mL of a radioisotope tracer containing 125I-human serum albumin (HSA) or 131I-HSA. A total of 12 minutes is allowed to pass for the isotope to mix within the blood appropriately. Once 12 minutes pass, the first blood sample is taken, followed by another sample every 6 minutes until there is a total of 5 specimens.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Blood Plasma -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Compartment volumes are measured based on the volume of distribution of tracer. When measuring plasma volume, the albumin-bound tracer, i.e., Evans blue, is used. As albumin tends to continuously leak out of the circulation, the tracer concentration is measured at serial intervals and is plotted on a logarithmic curve. This curve is then extrapolated to identify a “zero time” that allows the estimation of a virtual volume of distribution. The volume of distribution measured is the volume of plasma.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Water Balance -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma volume (PV)—This can be calculated using radioiodinated serum albumin (RISA) or Evans Blue dye, as they are specific to the plasma space.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Blood Plasma -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma can be measured using marker substances like radioactive iodine (131 I) and Evans blue (T-1824). Evans blue is the commonly used marker substance (aka tracer) since it binds strongly with albumin. The concept behind using a tracer is to use one that is well distributed in the compartment of interest. A known amount of tracer is introduced into the compartment, and its volume of distribution is measured. [4] Volume = Amount of Tracer/Concentration of Tracer

retrieved_docs.title: Technetium-99m -- Administration -- Technetium-99m Macroaggregated Albumin
retrieved_docs.content: Lung scintigraphy: 37 to 148 MBq (1 to 4 mCi) Portovenous shunt: 37 to 111 MBq (1 to 3 mCi) [13] Dosages may be modified depending on the patient and indications of imaging. Patients who receive the agent orally must fast for at least 6 hours before administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Albumin Colloid -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The principal mechanism of action of albumin infusion is to increase the colloid osmotic pressure. Albumin drives the interstitial fluid into the intravascular compartment and increases the effective volume of the circulatory system. [3] For patients undergoing large-volume paracentesis, albumin helps prevent a further decrease in effective arterial blood volume. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Body Fluids -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Crystalloid fluid resuscitation offers complications as it alters the ionic load of the serum. Specifically, normal saline replacement may lead to non-gap hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis. One-half normal saline, if not monitored closely, may dilute ionic components, leading to hyponatremia or, less often, hypokalemia. Abdominal compartment syndrome in septic shock patients is possibly secondary to fluid overload with the subsequent leak of fluid from capillaries into extravascular spaces. Colloid fluid resuscitation has its risks as well. The 2 major colloids used are albumen and hydroxyethyl starch. In the SAFE trial, which compared 4% albumin fluid with 0.9% normal saline, it was determined that outcomes were equivalent. However, in specific cases involving neurological injury, 4% albumin has an increased mortality rate compared to normal saline. As such, albumin should be avoided in this situation. Hydroxyethyl starch was studied in comparison and found to carry an increased risk of death or end-stage renal failure when compared to lactated Ringer's solution when used in sepsis patients. [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Albumin Colloid -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the appropriate clinical indications for albumin colloid therapy based on patient assessment and medical history. Differentiate between albumin colloid and other intravenous fluid options, considering their composition, indications, and potential adverse effects. Screen patients for contraindications or conditions requiring special consideration before implementing albumin colloid therapy. Implement the monitoring and management of patients receiving albumin colloid therapy, ensuring appropriate follow-up and timely adjustments. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Albumin Colloid -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Participants explore the normal blood plasma concentration range of 3.5 to 5 g/dL, with 60% of total albumin in the interstitial space. Beyond its fundamental physiological roles, this activity sheds light on albumin colloid's significance as a pharmaceutical therapeutic intervention and as a biomarker for diagnosing a spectrum of medical conditions. Learners gain insights into the multifaceted applications of albumin colloid, extending beyond its primary role as a predominant blood protein.

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Volume Study -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Measurement of plasma volume using 125I-HSA or 131I-HSA can lead to high radiation doses being taken up by the thyroid once the radioisotopes are broken down and reabsorbed. The recommendation is to block this uptake by the thyroid by saturating the binding sites with non-radioactive iodine before the volume study. [5] This can be done with potassium iodide 30mg/day for 3 to 4 weeks or with a one-time higher dosage of 400 mg.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Body Fluids -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Liver disease is also capable of inducing edema. This is due to a failure to produce osmotically active proteins—specifically, a failure to produce albumin. Albumin is found physiologically primarily in the plasma of the extracellular blood. It is found in lower concentrations in the interstitial space. As such, a decrease in body albumin directly decreases the “pull” of osmotic pressure into the capillaries. According to Starling forces, this results in the fluid moving into the interstitial spaces. [6] Additionally, fluid overload can be iatrogenically induced by excessive fluid replacement via intravenous access.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatopulmonary Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Radioactive Lung Perfusion Scanning
retrieved_docs.content: Radioactive lung perfusion scanning is another method used to assess pulmonary vessel dilatation, although this modality is less sensitive than contrast-enhanced echocardiography. Radioactive lung perfusion scanning does not distinguish between intrapulmonary and intracardiac shunting. However, this test may help decide if hepatopulmonary syndrome is contributing to hypoxemia in patients with concomitant lung disease. Radiolabeled albumin aggregates measuring approximately 20 µm in diameter are infused into the peripheral vein. Typically, particles of this size are trapped in the pulmonary microvasculature, and scintigraphy reveals nearly complete uptake in the lungs. When significant intrapulmonary shunting is present, a fraction of the albumin passes through the pulmonary vasculature and into the systemic circulation. Scintigraphy can reveal uptake in organs other than the lungs, which allows for the calculation of the shunt fraction. Brain shunt fraction >6% is considered significant.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Water Balance -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Extracellular fluid volume (ECFV)—To measure this volume, labeled inulin, sucrose, mannitol, or sulfate can be injected. These large molecules are impermeable to the cell membrane and only diffuse to the plasma and interstitial spaces.

retrieved_docs.title: Albumin Colloid -- Administration -- Specific Patient Population
retrieved_docs.content: Older patients: There is insufficient data from clinical studies to determine whether individuals 65 and older respond differently to albumin 5% compared to younger subjects. The use of albumin 25% in this age group should be limited based on need.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Autoradiografie, diagnostické techniky radioizotopové a diagnostické techniky otologické jsou všechny metodiky, u nichž lze uplatnit přístrojové vybavení jako povolený kvalifikátor. Stejně tak náhražky mléka, jiné respirační terapie, peritoneální dialýza a obecně i přístrojové vybavení samotné mohou být kvalifikovány pomocí přístrojového vybavení, což zahrnuje i obecnou anestezii (التخدير العام). Přístrojové vybavení může být dále specifikováno scintilací – počítáním a emisní počítačovou tomografií, čímž se rozšiřuje jeho využití v nukleární medicíně. Non‑imaging nuclear medicine probe or assay představuje centrální prvek, který je spojen s autoradiografií a má širší vztah k diagnostickým technikám radioizotopovým. Tento typ sondy nebo assay je součástí nukleární medicíny a je také propojen s scintilací – počítáním a emisní počítačovou tomografií. K jeho funkci jsou využívány radionuklidy jako látky, které jsou v tomto kontextu používány. Celkově tedy přístrojové vybavení slouží jako klíčový kvalifikátor pro širokou škálu diagnostických a terapeutických postupů – od autoradiografie a radioizotopových diagnostických technik po otologické vyšetření, peritoneální dialýzu, respirační terapii a obecnou anestezii – a je úzce propojeno s neobrazovými sondami a assayemi nukleární medicíny, které využívají radionuklidy a jsou podporovány metodami jako scintilace – počítání a emisní počítačová tomografie.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic radioisotopes constitute a specialized group within the broader class of radiopharmaceuticals (radiofarmaka) that are employed for imaging and functional assessment in nuclear medicine. Among these agents, technetium‑99m–based compounds are prominent, including 99mTc‑technecistan sodný, colloidal sulfur labeled with 99mTc, technetium‑99m dimerkaptojantarová acid, technetium‑99m disofenin, technetium‑99m exametazim, technetium‑99m lidofenin, technetium‑99m medronát, and technetium‑99m‑aggregated albumin. Additional diagnostic radioisotopes comprise indium‑111, sodium iodide I‑123, potassium‑42 chloride, potassium‑43 chloride, and carbon‑14. Carbon‑14 functions as a parent isotope for carbon‑and‑carbon‑compound‑containing products that are used in diagnostic applications. Collectively, these diagnostic radioisotopes enable clinicians to visualize physiological processes, assess organ function, and detect disease, thereby supporting accurate diagnosis and informed patient‑care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 295
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic radioisotopes comprise a diverse group of radiopharmaceuticals used in nuclear medicine imaging to assess physiological and pathological processes. Among them, potassium‑42 chloride and potassium‑43 chloride are listed as child agents of the diagnostic radioisotope category, indicating their role as potassium‑based imaging compounds. Indium‑111, including its specific form indium In‑111 oxyquinoline, is also classified as a diagnostic radioisotope, providing gamma‑emitting capabilities for scintigraphic studies. Sodium iodide I‑123 is another child of the diagnostic radioisotope group, widely employed for thyroid imaging due to its favorable photon energy and half‑life. Colloidal sulfur labeled with 99mTc represents a technetium‑based colloid used for reticuloendothelial system imaging. A series of technetium‑99m (99mTc) labeled compounds are identified as child agents of diagnostic radioisotopes, each designed for targeted imaging: technetium‑99m dimerkaptojantaric acid, technetium‑99m disofenin, technetium‑99m exametazim, technetium‑99m lidofenin, technetium‑99m medronate, and technetium‑99m aggregated albumin. These agents exploit the favorable physical properties of 99mTc to visualize specific organs or biological pathways, such as bone metabolism (medronate) or vascular and tissue perfusion (aggregated albumin). Additionally, 99mTc‑technecistan sodium is explicitly identified as a diagnostic radioisotope, further expanding the repertoire of technetium‑based imaging agents. Collectively, these diagnostic radioisotopes and their specialized derivatives enable clinicians to obtain functional imaging data, guide diagnosis, monitor disease progression, and inform therapeutic decisions across a wide range of medical specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 736
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic radioisotopes comprise a diverse group of radioactive compounds used for imaging and functional assessment in nuclear medicine. Specific members of this group include 99mTc‑technecistan sodný, Calcium‑45, Potassium‑42 chloride, Potassium‑43 chloride, indium‑111, colloidal sulfur labeled 99mTc, sodium iodide I‑123, technetium‑99m dimercaptoantaric acid, technetium‑99m disofenin, technetium‑99m exametazim, technetium‑99m lidofenin, technetium‑99m medronate, and technetium‑99m aggregated albumin. Each of these isotopes functions as a child entity under the broader category of diagnostic radioisotopes, reflecting their shared purpose of providing clinically valuable diagnostic information while differing in physical half‑life, emission characteristics, and target organ specificity. Together, they enable clinicians to visualize physiological processes, assess organ function, and detect disease with high sensitivity, thereby playing a central role in patient evaluation and management.

Overall, these entities illustrate a hierarchical and functional network in which broad nuclear medicine categories (such as الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة and other nuclear medicine imaging) give rise to specialized diagnostic procedures (e.g., radioisotope studies of the respiratory system and central nervous system), targeted imaging actions (e.g., radionuclide imaging methods), and therapeutic applications (e.g., therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine). This integrated structure supports comprehensive patient care by linking diagnostic imaging, functional assessment, and therapeutic interventions within the field of nuclear medicine.
